exp: Manual hyperinflation is a technique specifically used in managing mechanically ventilated patients.

q: Active cycle of breathing technique ACBT is carried out in\....
exp: ACBT is carried out in 3 phases (breathing control, thoracic expansion, forced expiration).

q: Manual clearing technique involves\....
exp: Chest physiotherapy is a manual technique used for secretion clearance.

q: Clinical presentation of critical care acquired weakness except
opts: Present about one week into the critical illness
exp: Sensation is typically preserved in critical illness weakness (a predominantly motor problem), unlike the other listed motor features.

q: Intra-abdominal hypertension is:
opts: When IAP is consistently \>12 mmHg with organ dysfunction
When IAP is \>12 mmHg without organ dysfunction
When IAP is consistently \<12 mmHg with organ dysfunction
When IAP is consistently \> 5 mmHg without organ dysfunction
exp: Intra-abdominal hypertension is defined as sustained IAP >12mmHg WITHOUT necessarily needing organ dysfunction to be present.

q: Abdominal compartment syndrome is:
opts: When an intra-abdominal pressure is \>20 mmHg with or without abdominal perfusion pressure \<50 mmHg
When an intra-abdominal pressure is \>20 mmHg with or without abdominal perfusion pressure greater than 50 mmHg
When an intra-abdominal pressure is 10-15 mmHg with normal abdominal perfusion pressure
exp: Abdominal compartment syndrome is defined by IAP >20mmHg with or without reduced abdominal perfusion pressure.

q: Diagnosis of abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS)
opts: Imaging is very helpful in the diagnosis of ACS
Imaging is not helpful in the diagnosis of ACS
Chest x-ray may show increased lung volumes
Chest x-ray may show depressed hemi-diaphragm
exp: Imaging has limited specific diagnostic value for ACS — clinical/pressure measurement is key, making 'not helpful' the accurate statement.

q: Intra-abdominal pressure can be measured directly using\....
exp: Intra-vesical (bladder) pressure measurement is the standard method to indirectly measure IAP.

q: The following is a secondary abdominal compartment syndrome.
exp: Septic shock (a non-abdominal originating cause) is a classic secondary cause of abdominal compartment syndrome.

q: The following are indication of monitoring intra-abdominal pressure except:
exp: A laparotomy closed under normal tension is not itself a high-risk indication for IAP monitoring, unlike shock, damage control surgery, or major burns.

q: Goal for a child with respiratory infection:  \"The child will exhibit adequate oxygenation and \_\_\_ airway.  Keep O2 saturation \>92-93%.
exp: The goal wording specifically calls for a patent (open, clear) airway.

q: Obstruction of the airway in the patient with asthma is caused by all of the following except:
opts: Contraction of muscles surrounding the bronchi
exp: Alveolar wall destruction is a feature of emphysema, not the airway obstruction mechanism in asthma.

q: On taking over in ICU from afternoon nurses, you discovered that a bottle of drug was not labelled and no information from handing over Nurse, what is the possible cause of this lapse?
opts: Lack of labeling from the pharmacy that dispense the drugs
Improper handing and taking over from previous shift
The patient relatives did not communicate to the Nurses
exp: Improper handover between shifts is the most likely cause of missing drug labelling/information.

q: You discovered that a patient's IV Cannula is issued but available cannulas are extreme sizes, what is the likely problem?
opts: Those are the sizes available in the pharmacy
They are the one used in ICU
exp: Only having extreme cannula sizes available, without appropriate mid-range options, points to poor inventory management.

q: All the following are objectives of Health Talk Except
exp: Misleading the audience is the opposite of a health talk's real objectives (reaching, engaging, and gaining acceptance from the audience).

q: The following are hindrances to effective Health Talk Except
opts: Pictures containing unfamiliar or strange subjects
exp: Using simple language helps (not hinders) effective health talks — it's the exception among the listed barriers.

q: A method of Teaching includes all the following Except....
exp: "Tertiary" isn't a recognised teaching method, unlike explanation, lecturing, and demonstration.

q: Which of this is not a major school of thought of management
exp: "Economic thought" isn't typically listed as a major management school, unlike classical, human resource, and modern approaches.

q: All the following are Rules of Management except
exp: Equity is a principle (per Fayol), not one of the specific 'rules' category being distinguished here from foresight/organisation/coordination.

q: All the followings are Principles of Management except
exp: Command is not one of Fayol's core numbered principles, unlike division of work, scalar chain, and unity of direction.

q: Fluid resuscitation requirements include the following except
exp: "Induction requirement" is not a recognised category of fluid resuscitation need, unlike deficit, ongoing loss, and maintenance.

q: Mr Wazobia is a 5 yea rs' old who presented with diarrhea and vomiting, which of these fluid is the best choice?
exp: Ringer's lactate is generally preferred for significant paediatric dehydration/fluid resuscitation over the other options given.

q: Which of these is not a characteristic of Sickle Red Cell
opts: Life span of 20 days or less
exp: Sickle cells are crescent-shaped, not the normal biconcave disc shape of healthy red cells.

q: A sickle cell disease patient presented with chest pain, low Spo2, cough, dyspnea, which of these is the cause?
exp: Chest pain, low SpO2, cough, and dyspnoea in sickle cell disease describe acute chest syndrome.

q: 10 year boys on admission with diagnosis of leukemia, which of these method/route of drugs administration will you avoid?
exp: Intramuscular injections are avoided in leukaemia patients due to bleeding/infection risk from low platelets/neutrophils.

q: Which one of these treatments of leukemia is still under investigation?
exp: Gene therapy for leukaemia remains an area of ongoing investigation/research rather than standard established treatment.

q: A disorder characterized by the accumulation of malignant leucocyte cells in the bone marrow and peripheral blood is\....
exp: Malignant leucocyte accumulation in bone marrow/blood defines leukaemia.

q: Nursing diagnosis as it relates to acute pain in renal trauma setting involves the following except
opts: Assessing for level of pain, location of pain
Monitoring of pain level closely and reporting increase in severity to the physician.
exp: Encouraging deep breathing is a general comfort measure, not specifically part of the pain-focused nursing diagnosis assessment listed.

q: Nursing care for patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) includes the following except:
opts: Managing complications such as urinary tract infection
exp: Menstrual assessment is irrelevant to BPH care, which affects only male patients.

q: Functional causes of urinary tract obstruction include:
exp: Detrusor-sphincter dysynergia is a functional (neuromuscular coordination) cause of urinary obstruction, unlike the mechanical causes listed.

q: Nursing intervention in patient with urinary retention includes all the following except:
opts: Provision of appropriate catheter care when catheter is present
Teach self-catheterization and instruct in use and care of indwelling catheters
Administer intravenous furosemide to encourage voiding
exp: IV furosemide would worsen retention-related problems, not relieve retention — it does not belong among appropriate retention interventions.

q: Immediate attention by urologist is required in the following conditions except:
opts: Children with obstruction in a solitary kidney
exp: A urine output of 30-50ml/hour in an adult is essentially normal and does not represent an urgent urological emergency.

q: Complications of penile trauma include all the following except:
exp: Peyronie's disease is a chronic condition, not a typical direct acute complication of penile trauma.

q: All the following are critical care considerations in bladder trauma except:
opts: Immediate priority to airway control, maintenance of ventilation, oxygenation and perfusion.
Trauma leader is important to coordinate management in multiply injured patient
Complete in depth assessment of patient is required after immediately treating life threatening injuries
exp: Avoiding repeated assessment is unsafe — ongoing reassessment is essential in trauma care, making this the incorrect approach.

q: The following are nursing interventions in urological trauma except
opts: Checking specific areas like ribs and flank for swelling and marking with permanent marker for monitoring progression.
Collection of urine samples and ensuring proper labeling
Carrying out venous cut-down procedure to secure IV line
Preparing patient for Ultrasound scan and CT scan
exp: Venous cut-down isn't a standard urological trauma-specific nursing intervention, unlike the assessment/documentation tasks listed.

q: The method and process involved in facilitating peaceful ending of conflict and retribute defines
exp: The process of peacefully ending conflict is conflict resolution.

q: The following are examples of family stressors except.
exp: Land allocation is not a typically cited core family stressor, unlike marital discord, finances, and child-parent issues.

q: Suicide is when people \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ themselves with the aim of ending their life.
exp: Suicide involves a person harming themselves with intent to end their life.

q: Family stress can show up as the following except.
exp: Illness itself is a stressor cause, not a sign that family stress is showing up, unlike missed commitments, anger, or arguments.

q: One of the following is called the positive stress.
exp: Eustress is the recognised term for positive, beneficial stress.

q: All are cognitive symptoms of psychological Trauma except.
exp: Exhaustion is more a physical/emotional symptom than a cognitive one, unlike memory loss, nightmares, and disorientation.

q: Eustress stress means
exp: Eustress describes a state of positive energisation from stress.

q: The following are Caregiver stress management in the family except.
opts: Asking for and accepting help when you need it
Taking the training you need to help your loved one
Working hard to pay the family bills
Making time to take breaks every day
exp: Overworking to pay bills adds to caregiver stress rather than managing it — the opposite of a coping strategy.

q: The following are children stress management except.
opts: Eating nutritious foods, like protein and vegetables
exp: Worrying about problems is the opposite of healthy stress management, unlike talking, exercise, and good nutrition.

q: The three types of eclampsia include the following except Ante partum Intra partum Pre partum Post-partum ANS; C The following are common symptoms of eclampsia except
exp: Diabetes is not a recognised symptom of eclampsia, unlike seizures, loss of consciousness, and agitation.

q: All are management of eclampsia fit except
exp: Early ambulation is inappropriate during an acute eclamptic emergency, unlike airway/oxygen/aspiration-prevention measures.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is one of the principles of treatment of eclampsia
exp: Preventing further convulsions is a fundamental principle of eclampsia treatment.

q: The following are complications of eclampsia except
exp: Sepsis is not a direct, classic complication of eclampsia itself, unlike neurological deficit, pulmonary oedema, and renal failure.

q: Severe eclampsia is a condition considered as follows except
opts: Can happen without signs of pre-eclampsia
exp: Severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia does not occur 'before' pre-eclampsia — pre-eclampsia precedes eclampsia, not the reverse.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is a drug given intramuscularly to control convulsion
exp: Magnesium sulphate is the standard IM/IV drug used to control eclamptic convulsions.

q: If pre-eclampsia worsens and affect the brain causing seizures, the patient have develop \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Pre-eclampsia progressing to seizures is by definition eclampsia.

q: The following is one of the criteria for diagnosing severe eclampsia
opts: Systolic blood pressure at 110 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure at 60 mmHg
Systolic blood pressure at 160 mmHg or higher or diastolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg or higher on 2 occasions at least 6 hours apart from bed rest
Systolic blood pressure at 130 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure at 100 mmHg on 2 occasions
exp: Sustained severe BP readings (≥160/100) on two occasions are diagnostic criteria for severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia.

q: Which of these is not associated with epiglottitis?
exp: Parasites are not a recognised cause of epiglottitis, unlike bacteria, viruses, and foreign bodies.

q: Regarding the newborn which of this is correct?
opts: Preterms are babies delivered before 40 weeks of delivery
All preterms are low birth weight
All preterms are small for gestational age
The term newborn may be small for gestational age
exp: A term (not preterm) baby can still be small for gestational age — the statement correctly reflects this distinction.

q: Regarding neonatal jaundice which of this is false
exp: Neonatal jaundice is actually more common in preterm (not term) babies, making that statement the false one.

q: Complications of hyperbilirubinemia include the followings except
exp: "White teeth" is not a complication of hyperbilirubinaemia — in fact, kernicterus can cause dental enamel discolouration, the opposite of what's stated.

q: In fetal circulation which of this is false?
opts: The lungs are filled with fluid
exp: There is only ONE umbilical vein (carrying oxygenated blood), not two, making that statement false.

q: A baby on phototherapy may develop the following except
exp: Diarrhoea is not typically listed as a phototherapy side effect, unlike fever, rash, and fluid loss/overload issues.

q: Which of the following statements is false?
opts: The neonatal period is the first week of life
Mortality is high in the neonatal period
exp: The neonatal period is actually the first 28 days (four weeks), not just the first week — that statement is false.

q: Treatment acute chest syndrome includes the followings except
exp: "Chest stimulation" is not a recognised acute chest syndrome treatment, unlike oxygen, incentive spirometry, and analgesia.

q: Examples of sickle cell disease is/are except
exp: Hb AS is sickle cell TRAIT (a carrier state), not sickle cell disease itself, unlike SS, SC, and other true disease genotypes.

q: Which of the following is not a treatment modality for patient with epiglottitis?
exp: Simple observation alone is inadequate for epiglottitis, which is a true airway emergency requiring active intervention.

q: All the following is correct about definition of disseminated intravascular coagulation except?
opts: There is a widespread activation of clotting cascade
There is a widespread formation of blood vessel throughout the body
exp: DIC is a pathological (not normal/physiologic) process of abnormal widespread clotting activation.

q: Concerning the following investigation in DIC, which is not correct?
exp: Fibrinogen is decreased (consumed), not increased, in DIC — making that statement incorrect.

q: The following are features of bronchial asthma except
exp: Angioedema is not a typical bronchial asthma feature, unlike chest tightness, breathlessness, and inability to speak in severe attacks.

q: The following drugs are useful in the management of bronchial asthma except
exp: Alpha blockers are not used to treat bronchial asthma, unlike beta-agonists, leukotriene modifiers, and anticholinergics.

q: The following structures are involved in epiglottitis except
exp: The uvula is part of the soft palate, not directly involved in epiglottitis, unlike the epiglottic/laryngeal structures listed.

q: Respiratory treatment that involves percussion, vibration, and postural drainage to mobilize secretions.  A cupped hand or special device over the chest wall/rib cage is used to break up secretions is called?
exp: Chest physiotherapy (CPT) combines percussion, vibration, and postural drainage to mobilise secretions.

q: In patients with Cystic Fibrosis, abnormally thick mucus leads to obstruction of the secretory ducts of the \_\_ that can contribute to malnutrition and diabetes.
exp: Thick mucus obstructing pancreatic ducts in cystic fibrosis contributes to malnutrition and diabetes.

q: Nurses and other healthcare providers often have difficulty helping a terminally ill patient through the necessary stages leading to acceptance of death. Which of the following strategies is most helpful to the nurse in achieving this goal?
opts: Reading books and other literature on the subject of thanatology.
Reflecting on the significance of death.
Reviewing varying cultural beliefs and practices related to death.
Taking psychology courses related to gerontology.
exp: Reflecting on death's significance helps nurses process their own feelings, which is key to supporting patients through acceptance.

q: The most important nursing intervention to correct skin dryness is
opts: Ask the physician to refer the patient to a dermatologist, and suggest that the patient wear home-laundered sleepwear.
Encourage the patient to increase his fluid intake, use non-irritating soap when bathing the patient, and apply lotion to the involved areas.
Avoid bathing the patient until the condition is remedied, and notify the physician.
Consult the dietitian about increasing the patient's fat intake, and take necessary measures to prevent infection.
exp: Increasing fluids, using gentle soap, and applying lotion directly addresses skin dryness at its source.

q: How can you Drag-and-drop and item in a folder
opts: to drag and drop, move the mouse pointer to that object and press the left mouse button once.
to drag and drop you press the left mouse button twice in quick succession.
to drag and drop just press the Right Mouse Button once.

to drag and drop a selected object, move the pointer to object you want to drag and drop, then press and hold the left mouse button, then move the object to where you want to drop it then release the left mouse button
exp: This describes the correct click-hold-drag-release technique for dragging and dropping an object.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ refers to the meaningful output or result obtained after processing data
exp: Information is defined as the meaningful output produced after processing raw data.

q: Which of the following conditions is most likely to directly cause peritonitis?
exp: A perforated ulcer spills GI contents directly into the peritoneal cavity, directly causing peritonitis.

q: A client with gastric cancer may exhibit which of the following symptoms?
exp: A feeling of fullness (early satiety) is a classic symptom of gastric cancer.

q: Fistulas are most common with which of the following bowel disorders?
exp: Crohn's disease characteristically causes transmural inflammation leading to fistula formation.

q: Which area of the alimentary canal is the most common location for Crohn's disease?
exp: The terminal ileum is the most common site affected by Crohn's disease.

q: Medical management of the client with diverticulitis should include which of the following treatments?
exp: Antibiotics are a core treatment for the infective/inflammatory component of diverticulitis.

q: Which of the following tests should be administered to a client suspected of having diverticulosis?
exp: Barium enema is used to visualise diverticula in the colon.

q: Which of the following complications is thought to be the most common cause of appendicitis?
exp: A fecalith (hardened stool) obstructing the appendix is the most common cause of appendicitis.

q: Which of the following factors can cause hepatitis A
opts: Sexual contact with an infected person.
exp: Hepatitis A spreads via the faecal-oral route, commonly through contaminated food.

q: The correct sequence for abdominal assessment is:
exp: Correct abdominal assessment order is inspection, auscultation, percussion, then palpation.

q: Peritonitis can occur as a complication of:
exp: Perforated peptic ulcer disease is a classic cause of secondary peritonitis.

q: Patients with esophageal varices would reveal the following assessment:
exp: Bleeding oesophageal varices cause blood loss, triggering a compensatory rise in heart rate.

q: Peptic ulcer disease may be caused by which of the following?
exp: H. pylori infection is a major recognised cause of peptic ulcer disease.

q: Nitrosocarcinogen production can be inhibited with the intake of:
exp: Vitamin C can inhibit the formation of carcinogenic nitroso compounds in the stomach.

q: Which of the following tests can be useful as a diagnostic and therapeutic tool in the biliary system?
exp: ERCP allows both diagnosis and therapeutic intervention (e.g. stone removal) in the biliary system.

q: To inhibit pancreatic secretions, which pharmacologic agent would you anticipate administering to a patient with acute pancreatitis?
exp: Somatostatin suppresses pancreatic secretion, reducing pancreatic autodigestion in acute pancreatitis.

q: A patient with severe cirrhosis of the liver develops hepatorenal syndrome. Which of the following nursing assessment data would support this?
opts: Weight gain of less than 1kg per week
exp: Oliguria with rising nitrogenous waste (azotaemia) is the hallmark of hepatorenal syndrome.

q: Which phase of hepatitis would the nurse incur strict precautionary measures at?
exp: The icteric (jaundice) phase is when infectivity/precautions are most critical in hepatitis.

q: You are caring for a 35 year old female patient in a hepatic coma. Which evaluation criteria would be the most appropriate?
opts: The patient demonstrates an increase in the level of consciousness.
The patient exhibits improved skin integrity.
The patient verbalizes decreased episodes of pain.
The patient experiences no evident signs of bleeding.
exp: Improved level of consciousness is the most direct, relevant marker of recovery from hepatic coma.

q: The critical care nurse recognizes that an ideal plan for caregiver involvement includes;
opts: a caregiver at the bedside at all times.
An individually devised plan to involve caregivers with care and comfort measures.
allowing caregivers at the bedside at preset, brief intervals.
restriction of visiting in the ICU because the environment is overwhelming to caregivers.
exp: An individualised plan tailored to each family's needs is the ideal approach to caregiver involvement.

q: Clients with chronic illnesses are more likely to get pneumonia when which of the following situations is present?
opts: dehydration and continuous loss of water
Living with many people in a small space
exp: Overcrowded living conditions increase pneumonia risk through greater exposure to pathogens.

q: Which of the following is a priority goal for the client with COPD?
opts: Increasing carbon dioxide levels in the blood.
exp: Maintaining functional ability is a central, realistic priority goal in chronic COPD management.

q: The nurse assesses the respiratory status of a client who is experiencing an exacerbation of COPD secondary to an upper respiratory tract infection. Which of the following findings would be expected?
exp: Coarse crackles and rhonchi are expected findings during a COPD exacerbation with infection.

q: A client has just returned to a nursing unit following bronchoscopy. A nurse would implement which of the following nursing interventions for this client?
opts: Encouraging additional fluids for the next 24 hours
Ensuring the return of the gag reflex before offering foods or fluids
exp: Confirming the gag reflex has returned prevents aspiration before resuming oral intake post-bronchoscopy.

q: A female client is scheduled to have a chest radiograph. Which of the following questions is of most importance to the nurse assessing this client?
opts: "Are you wearing any metal chains or jewelry?"
"Are you able to hold your arms above your head?"
"Is there any possibility that you could be pregnant?"
exp: Pregnancy status must always be checked before any radiographic exposure due to fetal radiation risk.

q: Aminophylline (theophylline) is prescribed for a client with acute bronchitis. A nurse administers the medication, knowing that the primary action of this medication is to:
opts: Relax smooth muscles of the bronchial airway.
exp: Aminophylline's primary action is relaxing bronchial smooth muscle (bronchodilation).

q: A patient was rushed to the clinic department due to hyperventilation. Which nursing intervention is the most appropriate for the client who is subsequently developing respiratory alkalosis?
exp: Slow, deep breathing helps a hyperventilating patient retain CO2 and correct respiratory alkalosis.

q: A patients test revealed that her serum calcium is 2.5 mEq/L. Which assessment data does the nurse document when a client diagnosed with hypocalcemia develops a carpopedal spasm after the blood-pressure cuff is inflated?
exp: Carpopedal spasm triggered by BP cuff inflation is specifically Trousseau's sign.

q: A patient with a very dry mouth, skin, and mucous membranes is diagnosed with dehydration. Which intervention should the nurse perform when caring for a patient diagnosed with fluid volume deficit?
opts: Obtaining the clients weight weekly at different times of the day.
Maintaining I V therapy at the keep vein open rate.
Monitoring arterial blood gas ABG results
exp: Tracking intake and output directly monitors fluid balance status in dehydration.

q: A female patient is diagnosed with hypomagnesemia, which nursing intervention would be appropriate?
opts: Instructing the client on the importance of preventing infection.
Instituting seizure precaution to prevent injury.
Teaching the client the importance of early ambulation.
Avoiding the use of tight tourniquet when drawing blood.
exp: Seizure precautions are essential since hypomagnesemia increases neuromuscular excitability/seizure risk.

q: OS computer abbreviation usually means ?
exp: OS stands for Operating System.

q: Which of the following hypertensive emergence is not correct
opts: Low blood pressure over hours to days
exp: A slow drop in BP over hours/days is not a hypertensive emergency — hypertensive emergencies involve rapid severe BP elevation, not low BP.

q: Concerning fulminant hepatic failure which is not a poor prognostic factor \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: A PT under 10 seconds difference (near normal) reflects preserved clotting function — a GOOD, not poor, prognostic sign.

q: Principles of radiation protection include all EXCEPT
exp: Space is not a standard cited radiation protection principle, unlike time, distance, and shielding/collimation.

q: What technical factor should always be checked before interpreting a frontal chest radiograph? \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Checking adequate inspiration is a standard technical check before reading a chest film.

q: Barium Based contrast studies include all the following EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Enterocysis (a small bowel study) is technically distinct from the barium swallow/enema/sulfate preparations being grouped here.

q: Management of uterine rupture is one of the following
exp: Simple surgical repair is the standard management approach for uterine rupture.

q: A 35year old para 7+2 \[6alive\] ,just had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a live baby boy, she is lying on bed not talking, on examination her pulse rate is 100b/m, blood pressure 50/?, the possible cause is: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: Not regular in antenatal visit prenatally
Inadequate history of onset of labor
exp: Persistent hypotension/tachycardia postpartum with unclear labour history raises suspicion for occult bleeding tied to inadequate labour monitoring/history.

q: Concerning magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) it should not be used in patients with: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Patients with metallic implants/devices cannot safely undergo MRI due to the strong magnetic field.

q: Which of these technical factors should always be checked first before interpreting a frontal chest radiograph include: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: Check hemidiaphragm if the patient has taken an adequate deep breath
Check the identity information at the top of the film to make sure it is the correct film
exp: Confirming patient identity on the film is a fundamental first check before any interpretation.

q: Principles of treatment of a severely malnourished child include all EXCEPT
exp: Treating/preventing COVID-19 is not part of the classic WHO severe malnutrition treatment principles (dehydration, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia).

q: One of these is NOT an output device \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: A keyboard is an input device, not an output device.

q: One of these is NOT a characteristic of a computer \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: "Data" itself is not a characteristic/property of a computer, unlike efficiency, portability, and accuracy.

opts: Data is the physical and tangible parts of a computer that we can see
Data is a set of instructions the computer follows to solve Problems
Data is the value of an attribute or characteristic belonging to an entity
Data is a group of Patients
exp: Data is defined as the value of an attribute/characteristic belonging to an entity.

q: The physical and tangible parts of a computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: The physical, tangible components of a computer are called hardware.

q: Water leaves the body by all the following mechanism EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: From feces eliminated from the intestine
Through metabolic reaction taking place in the cells
Through air expired from the lungs
Through sweat given off at the skin
exp: Water is lost via faeces, expired air, and sweat — metabolic reactions produce water rather than eliminate it, making that the exception.

q: The rate and depth of breathing has a regulatory influence on the: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: Rate at which fats are broken down in the body
Amount of water taken into the body
exp: Breathing rate/depth directly regulates CO2 elimination, controlling the body's acid-base balance.

q: Approximately one third of the body water exists in the: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: About two-thirds of body water is intracellular and one-third extracellular — the extracellular compartment holds roughly a third.

q: An example of a strong acid found in the body is: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Hydrochloric acid (gastric acid) is a classic strong acid found in the body.

q: The two basic methods requirement for optional physiologic functions of all cells include\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ and \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Respiration and excretion are the two basic processes required to maintain optimal cellular physiological function.

q: Concerning Mechanical ventilation, Phase variables are Parameters which control the Phases of a mechanical breath, which of the following is not a Phase
exp: Volume is a set (target) parameter, not one of the four defined ventilator phase variables (trigger, limit, cycle, baseline).

q: These are all essential steps in the Management of myocardial infarction EXCEPT\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Antibacterial therapy is not a core MI management step, unlike anti-ischaemic, anticoagulant, and antiplatelet therapy.

q: Indication for thrombolysis in myocardial infection includes the following EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
opts: New onset left bundle branch block
exp: Non-ST elevation MI is generally NOT an indication for thrombolysis, unlike STEMI, new LBBB, or posterior MI.

q: Concerning Mechanical ventilation, Phase variables are Parameters which control the Phases of a mechanical breath, which of the following is not a Phase
exp: Same repeated logic: Volume is not one of the defined phase variables (trigger/limit/cycle) of a mechanical breath.

q: What is Computer Memory (Storage devices)?
opts: They are devices that are used for storing data and information permanently or temporarily
They are devices that perform all the processing in the computer.
They are devices that convert information from the form that the computer understands to a form that humans can understand.
They are devices that command the computer on how to execute programs to solve problems
exp: Storage devices are used to hold data/information either temporarily or permanently.

q: One of these is NOT an Input Device
exp: A digital sphygmomanometer is a medical measuring device, not a standard computer input device like the others listed.

q: Software designed and installed on a computer intended to solve specific problems or perform particular tasks is called?
exp: Software built for a specific task/problem is application software.

q: One of these is Not an Output Device
exp: A keyboard is an input, not an output, device.

opts: manages the resources on the computer and enable the hardware to function properly
is used to manage health information of patients
exp: System software manages computer resources and enables hardware to function properly.

q: A set of instruction and associated documentation that the computer follows to solve a problem is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: A set of instructions the computer follows, plus documentation, is software.

q: The devices that give out data and information from the computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: Devices that give out data/information from the computer are output devices.

q: The devices that send data and control signals INTO the computer is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: Devices sending data/control signals into the computer are input devices.

q: The physical and tangible parts of a computer called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: The physical, tangible parts of a computer are called hardware.

q: all of these are Computer hardware EXCEPT\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: Software/programs are not hardware — they are the exception among the physical components listed.

q: What is information?
opts: Information is the first step in data collection
Information is the meaningful result of processing data.
exp: Information is the meaningful result produced from processing data.

q: What is an Entity?
opts: An Entity is any expression that can describe computer laboratory
An Entity is any person, object or idea about which data can be collected
An Entity is a collection of programs
An Entity is the equivalent of communication on a network
exp: An entity is any person, object, or idea about which data can be collected.

q: What is an attribute?
opts: An attribute is an object stored on a computer
An attribute is the smallest unit of a computer file
An attribute is a collection of related records
An attribute is a property or characteristic that can qualify or describe an entity
exp: An attribute is a property/characteristic that describes an entity.

q: What is Data?
opts: Data is a group of patients
Data is a set of instructions the computer follows to solve problems
Data is the value of an attribute or characteristic belonging to an entity
Data is the physical and tangible parts of a computer that we can see
exp: Data is the value of an attribute/characteristic belonging to an entity.

q: Which of these best describes a System?
opts: a group of components communicating with the user
a group of components presenting information
a group of components or elements organized for a common purpose or working together to achieve a goal
a group of components or elements creating software
exp: A system is a group of components organised together to achieve a common goal.

q: A network in which all the communicating computers are of the same or similar capacity is called a \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: A network of similarly capable computers communicating as equals is a peer-to-peer network.

q: in a Computer Network the means of connection is \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_?
exp: The medium/channel is the means of connection in a computer network.

opts: an application used for accessing resources on a computer network.
an application for creating richly formated documents
an application for manipulating numerical data arranged in rows and columns
exp: A web browser is the application used to access resources on a computer network (the internet).

q: What is a Computer Network?
opts: A Computer Network is the interconnection of schools to give information
A Computer Network is the interconnection people to increase productivity
A Computer Network is the interconnection of two or more computers or computing devices through a medium for the purpose of sharing resources.
A Computer Network is the interconnection of two or more agents to achieve a goal
exp: A computer network is the interconnection of two or more computing devices to share resources.

q: Which of these best defines a computer
opts: A computer is an electronic device that accepts data processes data stores data and gives out information.
A computer is an electronic communication device.
A computer is a music playing calculation device.
A computer is an education device for presentation.
exp: A computer is defined as an electronic device that accepts, processes, stores, and outputs data/information.

q: DB computer abbreviation usually means ?
exp: DB commonly stands for Database.

q: What is the primary goal of infection control practices in critical care?
exp: Preventing healthcare-associated infections is the primary goal of infection control in critical care.

q: Which of the following is a common sign of inadequate oxygenation in a critically ill patient?
exp: Cyanosis (bluish skin) is a classic visible sign of inadequate oxygenation.

q: What is the primary purpose of a nasogastric (NG) tube in critical care?
opts: C) Providing nutrition and medications via the stomach
exp: An NG tube's main critical care purpose is delivering nutrition and medication directly into the stomach.

q: In critical care, what is the primary role of a diuretic medication?
exp: Diuretics work primarily by promoting urine output.

q: What is the primary goal of sepsis management in critical care?
opts: C) Identifying and treating the infection promptly
exp: Prompt identification and treatment of the underlying infection is the core goal of sepsis management.

q: Which of the following is a common method for preventing ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in critical care?
opts: B) Frequent suctioning of the endotracheal tube
exp: Frequent endotracheal suctioning helps clear secretions and reduce VAP risk.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) in critical care?
opts: C) Evaluating neurological status and consciousness
exp: The GCS evaluates neurological status and level of consciousness.

q: Which of the following is a common complication of central line placements in critical care?
exp: Catheter-related bloodstream infection is a well-known complication of central line placement.

q: What is the primary goal of enteral nutrition in critical care?
opts: D) Delivering nutrition through the gastrointestinal tract
exp: Enteral nutrition delivers nutrients directly through the GI tract.

q: In critical care, what is the primary purpose of a pulmonary artery catheter (PAC)?
opts: D) Assessing cardiac function and pressures
exp: A pulmonary artery catheter assesses cardiac function and intracardiac/pulmonary pressures.

q: Which of the following is a common cause of acute pancreatitis in critical care patients?
exp: Gallstones and alcohol use are the two leading causes of acute pancreatitis.

q: What is the primary goal of a ventricular assist device (VAD) in critical care?
opts: To support heart function and circulation
exp: A VAD mechanically supports heart function and circulation in severe heart failure.

q: Which of the following is a common cause of stress ulcers in critical care patients?
exp: Severe illness and physiological stress are the classic drivers of stress ulcer development in critical care.

q: What is the primary purpose of a Swan-Ganz catheter in critical care?
exp: A Swan-Ganz (pulmonary artery) catheter is used to monitor cardiac hemodynamics.

q: In critical care, what is the primary role of a respiratory therapist?
exp: Respiratory therapists provide respiratory support and therapies in critical care.

q: Which of the following file extensions is commonly associated with image files?
exp: .jpg is a standard image file extension.

q: What does a spreadsheet software like Microsoft Excel allow users to do?
opts: Create, organize, and analyze data in a tabular format
exp: Excel allows creating, organising, and analysing data in a tabular (spreadsheet) format.

q: What is a web browser primarily used for?
exp: A web browser is primarily used to browse the internet.

q: Which of the following is an example of an electronic spreadsheet software?
exp: Microsoft Excel is a well-known electronic spreadsheet application.

q: What is cloud computing?
opts: A technology that allows accessing and storing data and programs over the internet
exp: Cloud computing allows accessing/storing data and programs over the internet rather than locally.

q: A 16-year old patient with cystic fibrosis is admitted with increased shortness of breath and possible pneumonia. Which nursing activity is most important to include in the patient's care?
opts: Perform postural drainage and chest physiotherapy every 4 hours.
Allow the patient to decide whether she needs aerosolized medications.
Place the patient in a private room to decrease the risk of further infection.
Plan activities to allow at least 8 hours of uninterrupted sleep.
exp: Regular postural drainage and chest physiotherapy is essential to clear thick CF secretions and reduce infection/respiratory compromise.

q: If a person has AB- blood, who can donate blood to him?
exp: AB- can only safely receive Rh-negative blood of any ABO type, since anti-Rh antibodies would react to any Rh+ blood.

q: On what basis is the blood group classified?
exp: Blood groups are classified based on antigens present on RBCs and antibodies in plasma.

q: Other than transfusion, when is it necessary to take the Rh Factor into consideration?
exp: Rh factor compatibility is critical in pregnancy due to risk of maternal-fetal Rh incompatibility.

q: A person has blood group A means he/she has...................
exp: Blood group A means the person has the A antigen on their red blood cells.

q: Which of the following is the correct sequence in which urine flows through the kidney toward the urinary bladder?
opts: Renal pelvis, major calyx, minor calyx, papillary duct, ureter.
Papillary duct, minor calyx, major calyx, renal pelvis, ureter.
Minor calyx, major calyx, papillary duct, renal pelvis, ureter.
Papillary duct, major calyx, minor calyx, ureter, renal pelvis.
exp: Urine flows: minor calyx → major calyx → renal pelvis → ureter → bladder.

q: The bladder is lined with \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ epithelium.
exp: The bladder is lined with transitional epithelium, which allows stretching.

q: Each kidney receives blood from..................
exp: Each kidney receives its blood supply from the renal artery.

q: The maximum volume of air contained in the lung by a full forced inhalation is called \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.
exp: Maximum air the lungs can hold after full inhalation is total lung capacity.

q: Adduction is?
opts: Movement of a body part away from the mid-line
Movement of a body part towards the mid-line
Movement of a body part forwards
movement of a body part backwards
exp: Adduction is movement of a body part toward the midline.

q: Which enzyme digests nucleic acids?
exp: Nucleases digest nucleic acids (DNA/RNA).

q: Endocrine system consist of the following glands EXCEPT:
exp: The spleen is a lymphatic organ, not an endocrine gland.

q: Protective functions of the skin are the following EXCEPT:
exp: Protecting against insects is not a recognised protective skin function, unlike chemical/water-loss protection.

q: The layer of the skin that provides protection against bacteria, as well as chemical and mechanical injuries is the:
exp: The epidermis is the outer barrier layer protecting against bacteria and injury.

q: Which of this part of alimentary canal accumulates lymphatic tissue?
exp: The appendix contains significant lymphatic tissue.

q: The followings are accessory organs of digestion EXCEPT:
exp: Lymph nodes are not accessory digestive organs, unlike salivary glands, teeth, and gall bladder.

q: Delirium Risk Factors are as follows EXCEPT
exp: Obesity is not a classic delirium risk factor, unlike dementia, fractures, and infections.

q: Communication may take a ................ to master
exp: Effective communication is a skill that can take a lifetime to truly master.

q: If healthy behaviour is any activity undertaken by a person believing himself to be healthy, illness behaviour is any activity undertaken by a person who feels ................
exp: Illness behaviour is activity undertaken by someone who feels ill.

q: Stress is the internal pressure while stressor is an:
exp: A stressor is an external tormentor/trigger; stress is the internal response/pressure.

q: In the treatment of alcohol withdrawal there are three line management
opts: Medication, Special therapy and Group therapy
exp: Alcohol withdrawal management follows supportive care, medical procedures, and medication together.

q: PTSD means
exp: PTSD stands for Post Traumatic Stress Disorder.

q: All are cognitive symptoms of psychological Trauma EXCEPT:
exp: Exhaustion is more physical than cognitive, unlike memory loss, nightmares, and disorientation.

q: What are the three Elements of Attitude?
exp: The three elements of attitude are Cognitive, Behavioural, and Affective.

q: Divergent thinking is to \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ as convergent thinking is to \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.
exp: Divergent thinking generates many solutions; convergent thinking narrows to one.

q: The Hormone produced by adrenal glands that arouses the body is ...
exp: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is the adrenal hormone that arouses the fight-or-flight response.

q: Positioning in critical care unit means the following EXCEPT
opts: Each position carries some degree of risk to the patient
Magnified in risk for patients who cannot /may not communicate.
To conform with patients demand absolutely
exp: Positioning should be based on clinical need/risk assessment, not absolute compliance with patient demand.

q: Unconsciousness include all the following EXCEPT
opts: A state of abnormal cerebral activity
Unresponsive to internal and external environmental changes
exp: Awareness of self and environment is the opposite of unconsciousness, not a feature of it.

q: In assessment of unconscious patient which of this is the ideal sequence used?
exp: ABCDE is the standard structured assessment sequence for an unconscious patient.

q: The following are criteria for CCU admission EXCEPT
exp: Chronic renal failure alone is not typically an acute CCU admission criterion, unlike arrest, MI, or major surgery.

q: The ideal instrument for examining the pupils for size and reaction is..
exp: A pen torch light is the standard bedside tool for pupillary examination.

q: Airway obstruction is diagnosed via the following EXCEPT
exp: Apnoea (absence of breathing) doesn't itself indicate airway obstruction sounds, unlike gurgling/snoring/drooling which reflect a partially obstructed airway.

q: Coughing during suctioning indicates stimulation of cough reflex centre at the
exp: The carina is highly sensitive and its stimulation during suctioning triggers a strong cough reflex.

q: The following are symptoms of Tension pneumothorax EXCEPT
exp: Tension pneumothorax causes falling (not rising) blood pressure due to impaired venous return.

q: Indications for chest tube insertion with underwater-seal drainage are the following EXCEPT
exp: Emphysema alone isn't a typical indication for chest tube/underwater seal drainage, unlike pneumothorax or haemopneumothorax.

q: Modifiable Risk Factors for MI include all EXCEPT
exp: Age is non-modifiable, unlike smoking, hypertension, and diabetes.

q: If the plan is to have one critical care unit in the hospital it should be located near:
exp: A single CCU is ideally located near the Accident & Emergency unit for rapid access to critical patients.

q: Myocardial oxygen demand is increased by the followings EXCEPT
exp: Sleep reduces (not increases) myocardial oxygen demand, unlike hypertrophy, fever, or hyperthyroidism.

q: The donated organ is called...
exp: The transplanted organ itself is called the graft.

q: The major aim of arteriovenous fistula is...
opts: Join artery and vein together for a better dialysis
Causes extra pressure and extra blood flow to the vein, making it grow larger and stronger
Make artery grow larger, stronger and extra blood flow to the vein
exp: An AV fistula’s purpose is to increase pressure/flow into the vein, making it enlarge and strengthen for dialysis access.

q: A protective layer of white cheese-like substance on a baby skin after birth is called....
exp: The white cheese-like newborn skin coating is vernix caseosa.

q: In peritoneal dialysis, the dialysate is
exp: Peritoneal dialysate is hyperosmolar to draw fluid/waste out of the blood by osmosis.

q: The chest tube placed high and posterior or lateral to drain
exp: A chest tube placed high/anterior is positioned to drain air.

q: In ............... situation, a one way value is inserted through the chest wall to treat air leaks
exp: A one-way (Heimlich) valve is used specifically in situations like haemothorax/pneumothorax to release air/fluid without letting it back in.

q: A patient develops a urinary tract infection after an indwelling urinary catheter has been inserted. This would most accurately be termed as which type of infection?
exp: An infection caused by a medical device/procedure is termed iatrogenic.

q: All the followings are aseptic barriers except?
exp: Antiseptic wipes are a cleaning measure, not a physical aseptic barrier like gloves, gowns, masks, or drapes.

q: Sepsis has dramatically increased by an unusual significant rate, over the past decade, due to the following reasons EXCEPT:
opts: Due to Cultures of the people
exp: "Cultures of the people" is a vague non-clinical factor, unlike drug resistance, virulence, and ageing population which are cited sepsis-rise drivers.

q: Asthma is a respiratory pathology that belongs to a group of respiratory diseases known as
exp: Asthma is classified among obstructive lung diseases.

q: Complications of Pumonary Embolism (PE)
exp: Antithrombin V deficiency isn't a real described complication of PE (it's not even a real named clotting factor), unlike cardiac death, obstructive shock, or cor pulmonale.

q: \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- is a standard healthcare practice that helps prevent the transfer of germs to or from an open wound and other susceptible areas on a patient's body.
exp: Aseptic technique is the standard practice preventing germ transfer to/from open wounds.

q: Risk Factors for Asthma include the following EXCEPT:
exp: Diabetes is not a recognised asthma risk factor, unlike pets, smoking, and genetics.

q: Which of the following nursing interventions would be most important for determining fluid balance in a client with end-stage renal failure?
exp: Accurately measuring fluid intake and output is the most direct way to assess fluid balance in renal failure.

q: Which of these patient scenarios is most indicative of critical thinking?
opts: Administering pain relief medication according to what was given last shift
Asking a patient what pain relief methods, pharmacological and non-pharmacological, have
Offering pain relief medication based on physician orders
Explaining to the patient that his reports of severe pain are not consistent with the minor
exp: Actively assessing the patient's own pain-relief preferences reflects genuine critical thinking, rather than following routine or orders blindly.

q: Professional nurses are responsible for making clinical decisions to
opts: Prove traditional methods of providing nursing care to patients.
Take immediate action when a patient\'s condition worsens.
Apply clear textbook solutions to patients\' problems.
Formulate standardized care plans for groups of patients
exp: Taking prompt clinical action as a patient's condition changes reflects professional clinical decision-making.

q: The important determinant of health are EXCEPT
exp: "Other factor" is a vague non-answer, unlike environment, socioeconomic status, and health services which are recognised health determinants.

q: According to Abraham Maslow, what is the most important need that must be fulfilled first?
exp: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs as the most fundamental, needing fulfilment first.

q: This Vasopressor drug is neurotransmitter and a hormone
exp: Adrenaline (epinephrine) functions as both a neurotransmitter and a hormone.

q: Specific antidote in paracetamol poisoning is
exp: Acetylcysteine is the specific antidote for paracetamol overdose.

q: Drugs that are used in symptomatic bradycardia are\...\...
exp: Atropine and dopamine are used to manage symptomatic bradycardia.

q: Convert 500 milligrams to grams
exp: 500mg = 0.5g (dividing by 1000 to convert mg to g).

q: Convert 50 ml to litres
exp: 50ml = 0.05L (dividing by 1000 to convert ml to L).

q: Airway management is a priority in critical care nursing so as to maintain the following EXCEPT..
exp: Haemostasis (clotting) is not a direct airway management goal, unlike ventilation, oxygenation, and electrolyte balance which relate to overall stability.
Trismus (locked jaw) prevents proper LMA insertion, making it a contraindication.

q: Arrange the following in sequence: Induction of anaesthesia, premedication, pre anaesthetic assessment, pre anaesthetic visit
opts: Induction, premedication, pre anaesthetic visit and pre anaesthetic assessment
Induction of anaesthesia, premedication, pre anaesthetic visit and pre anaesthetic assessment
Pre anaesthetic visit, pre anaesthetic assessment, induction and premedication
Pre anaesthetic visit, pre anaesthetic assessment, premedication and induction of anaesthesia
exp: Correct order: pre-anaesthetic visit, pre-anaesthetic assessment, premedication, then induction.

q: All the following are indications for airway management EXCEPT
exp: Being on spirometry is a monitoring tool, not itself an indication needing airway intervention.

q: Which of the following is not true?
opts: Oropharyngeal airway may cause laryngeal spasm
Oropharyngeal airway may lead to desaturation
exp: An oropharyngeal airway can trigger gagging/vomiting (in a conscious patient), but doesn't typically stimulate a cough reflex the way suctioning does — making that statement false among these.

q: All are part of care of anaesthesia devise EXCEPT :
opts: Use of disinfectants on breathing circuit
exp: Glutaraldehyde use is generally discouraged/limited due to toxicity, making it NOT standard advocated device care.

q: What is the most common cause of the chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)?
exp: Cigarette smoking is overwhelmingly the most common cause of COPD.

q: Most of the carbon dioxide in the Blood is transported as?
exp: Most CO2 in the blood is transported as bicarbonate ion.

q: The general indications for mechanical ventilation include all of the following EXCEPT:
exp: Hyperventilation reflects the lungs working too well, the opposite of needing ventilator support, unlike hypoxaemia, acidosis, and apnoea.

q: High frequency ventilation has the advantage of delivering \...\..... and reducing the incidence of \...\...\...
opts: Large mean airway pressure; necrotizing tracheobronchitis
Low peak inspiratory pressure; air trapping
exp: High-frequency ventilation delivers very small tidal volumes, reducing barotrauma risk.

q: The following are indications for ventilating a patient EXCEPT:
opts: Apnea or impending inability to breath
exp: "Respiratory exercise" isn't itself an indication for ventilation, unlike failure, apnoea, or severe hypoxia.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is defined as an absence of ventilatory support 48 hours following an extubation.
exp: Weaning success is defined as sustained spontaneous breathing without support 48 hours post-extubation.

q: Normal End Tidal CO2
exp: Normal end-tidal CO2 is about 35-45mmHg.

q: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)
opts: CPR for the infant victim will be 30 compressions to 2 breaths.
CPR ratio for the adult will be 15 compressions to 2 breaths.
CPR for the neonate victim will be 30 compressions to 2 breaths.
CPR ratio for the child and infant will be 15 compressions to 2 breaths.
exp: Both child and infant CPR use a 15:2 ratio when performed by trained rescuers/two-rescuer scenarios.

q: The science of collectiong, organizing, analyzing, interpreting and presenting data to asist in making more effective decisions is known as:
exp: Statistics is the science of collecting, organising, analysing, and presenting data for decision-making.

q: The main methods of data collection include all the following EXCEPT:
exp: Plagiarism is an ethical violation, not a legitimate data collection method.

q: The following are measuring scales used in research EXCEPT:
exp: "Weighing scale" is a physical tool, not a research measurement scale like nominal/ordinal/interval.

q: In the \_\_\_\_\_\_\_research, the researcher has to use facts or information already available.
exp: Applied research uses existing facts/knowledge to solve practical problems.

q: Data processing involves the following steps EXCEPT:
exp: "Data processing" as typically taught includes editing, coding, and classification; tabulation is often treated as a distinct presentation step rather than a processing step in this framework.

q: The following are reasons for research communication EXCEPT
opts: Improve the dissemination and repackaging of research for continuing knowledge.
exp: Enhancing funding isn't a core purpose of research communication, unlike evaluating impact, building capacity, and improving dissemination.

q: The following are Not contained in Research title EXCEPT
exp: Abbreviations should generally be avoided in a research title, unlike essential keywords.

q: The hardest section to write in research is?
exp: The discussion section, requiring deep interpretation, is widely considered the hardest to write.

q: Research means.............. EXCEPT
exp: "To harmonize" is not a standard definition of research, unlike investigate, test, and re-examine.

q: The following factors can influence choice of research topic EXCEPT
exp: Duration alone isn't typically a primary factor guiding topic choice, unlike discipline, acceptability, and funding.

q: These are types of enteral feeding EXCEPT
exp: Peripheral parenteral nutrition is IV (not enteral/gut-based) feeding, so it doesn't belong among enteral feeding types.

q: All the changes that occur in the food from the time we eat it, to its use in the body and discarding of the waste matter are known as:
exp: The overall handling of food from intake to waste elimination is broadly termed the food process.

q: All these are measures the body adapts in order to provide energy during fasting/starvation EXCEPT.
exp: Glycolysis breaks down glucose (needing glucose supply) rather than being a fasting-adaptation energy source like lipolysis, gluconeogenesis, or ketosis.

q: \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- diet is one which meets in full all the nutritional needs of the person.
exp: An adequate diet meets all of a person's nutritional needs.

q: BMI of 25 -- 29.9 kg/m2 indicates
exp: A BMI of 25-29.9 is classified as overweight.

q: Which of the following is not an input device?
exp: A speaker is an output device, not an input device.

q: What is the smallest unit of information?
exp: A bit is the smallest unit of computer information.

q: Which of the following is an input device?
exp: A mouse is an input device, unlike plotter/printer/VDU which are outputs.

q: A computer is accurate, but if the result of a computation is false, what is the main reason for it?
exp: Incorrect data entry ("garbage in, garbage out") is the main cause of false computational results despite accurate hardware.

q: The location of a resource on the internet is given by its?
exp: A resource's internet location is given by its URL.

q: What is the mean of the Booting in the system?
exp: Booting means restarting/starting up a computer.
USB stands for Universal Serial Bus.

q: Which of the following is not considered hardware?
exp: An operating system is software, not hardware.

q: The term LAN stands for?
exp: LAN stands for Local Area Network.

q: In Arteriovenous fistula:
opts: There is mixture of arterial blood
There is mixture of venous blood
There is mixture of artery and capillary blood
There is mixture of arterial and venous blood
exp: An AV fistula creates a direct connection mixing arterial and venous blood flow.

q: The major aim of Arteriovenous fistula is...
opts: Join artery and vein together for a better dialysis
Causes extra pressure and extra blood flow to the vein, making it grow larger and stronger
Make artery grow larger, stronger and extra blood flow to the vein
exp: The AV fistula’s purpose is to boost pressure/flow into the vein so it strengthens for dialysis access.

q: The word processing feature that catches most random typographical errors and misspellings is known as \_\_\_\_\_.
exp: A spell checker catches typographical and spelling errors.

q: How is the data stored on the diskette?
exp: Data on a diskette is stored magnetically.

q: BIOS is used?
exp: BIOS is used by the operating system to initialise hardware at startup.

q: \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- is exclusively a sequential access storage device?
exp: Magnetic tape is a purely sequential-access storage device, unlike disks which allow random access.

q: Identify the five main components of a computer system in their order?
opts: CPU, CD-ROM, Mouse, Keyboard, Sound card
Memory, Video card, Monitor, Software, Hardware
Modem, Keyboard, Word Processor, Printer, Screen
CPU, Memory, System bus, Input, Output
exp: CPU, memory, system bus, input, and output are the five core computer system components.

q: Which type of topology is best suited for large businesses which must carefully control and coordinate the operation of distributed branch outlets?
exp: A hierarchical topology suits large organisations needing centralised control over distributed branches.

q: Which of the following transmission directions listed is not a legitimate channel?
exp: "Double Duplex" is not a real transmission mode, unlike simplex, half-duplex, and full-duplex.

q: \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- is a transmission medium that is most appropriate to carry data in a computer network that is exposed to electrical interferences
exp: Optical fibre is immune to electrical interference, unlike copper-based media.

q: A collection of hyperlinked documents on the internet forms the
exp: Hyperlinked documents across the internet make up the World Wide Web.

q: Software programs that allow you to legally copy files and give them away at no cost are called \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
exp: Public domain software can be freely copied and distributed at no cost.

q: .......................skills are the skills we used when engaged in face to face communication.
exp: Verbal communication skills are specifically used in face-to-face spoken interaction.

q: In children, grieving is
exp: Children's grieving process is generally understood to take longer than adult grieving.

q: Behavioural Science is defined by the American Psychological Association as:
opts: Discipline that uses systemic observation and experimentation in the scientific study of human and non-human actions and reactions.
A discipline in which humans relate with their environment.
Discipline of applied behavioural analysis which involves applying behavioural principles in practice setting.
exp: APA defines behavioural science as the systematic scientific study of human/non-human actions and reactions.

q: Condition marked by lack of agreement or harmony as described by Merriam Webster defines:
exp: Merriam-Webster's definition of lack of harmony/agreement matches conflict.

q: The following are main risk factors for suicide EXCEPT:
exp: Mania is not a classic listed suicide risk factor, unlike family history, chronic pain, and depression.

q: The fluid that passes through the lymphatic vessels
opts: Passes from the lymphatic vessels into the arteries
Enters the left ventricle of the heart through the right thoracic duct
Moves in a single direction toward the heart
exp: Lymph moves in one direction, toward the heart, through the lymphatic system.

q: Where is the spleen situated?
exp: The spleen is located in the abdominal cavity (upper left quadrant).

q: Submandibular lymph glands are present under the .........
exp: Submandibular lymph nodes sit under the jaw/chin area.

q: Lymphatic fluid is rich in ....
exp: Lymphatic fluid is rich in antibodies (part of immune defence).

q: The main function of erythrocytes is .....
exp: The main function of red blood cells (erythrocytes) is oxygen transport.

q: The Vitamin essential for blood clotting is\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Vitamin K is essential for the clotting cascade.

q: Which blood type is a universal donor?
exp: O negative is the universal red cell donor.

q: Muscles which attach to bones are called:
exp: Muscles attached to bones for movement are skeletal muscles.

q: Food movement is by alternating contractions of the muscle layers called
exp: Alternating muscle contractions moving food along the GI tract is peristalsis.

q: Rugae is internal folds of the mucosa present in the
exp: Rugae are the internal folds of the stomach mucosa.

q: Regions of the stomach are all the following EXCEPT:
exp: The cardioesophageal region is a junction area, not one of the stomach's own defined regions (fundus/body/pylorus).

q: Which enzyme completes digestion of starch?
exp: Pancreatic amylase completes starch digestion in the small intestine.

q: Which enzyme is responsible for digestion of fat?
exp: Lipase is the enzyme responsible for fat digestion.

q: Positions used in nursing patients the ICU/CCU include all the following EXCEPT
exp: Standing is not a nursing position used for critically ill patients, unlike prone, cardiac, and lateral.

q: The following are models of CCU admission EXCEPT
exp: "Diagnosis model" isn't a recognised standard CCU admission model, unlike objective parameters, prognostic, and prioritisation models.

q: It is essential that critical care services be the following EXCEPT
exp: "Comfortable" isn't a core system-level requirement, unlike efficiency, effectiveness, and affordability.

q: Intensive Care Unit started in ..........................
exp: Modern Intensive Care Units originated in the 1950s (polio epidemic era).

q: The following are history areas you will assess during patients assessment EXCEPT
exp: Detailed educational history isn't a standard core assessment area, unlike medical history categories.

q: Airway obstruction can be diagnosed via the following EXCEPT
exp: Stridor indicates partial obstruction but 'diagnosing via' points to physical exam signs like gurgling/snoring/drooling rather than an auditory sign like stridor in this specific framing.

q: ...................... supply the vocal cords
exp: The vagus nerve supplies motor function to the vocal cords via its recurrent laryngeal branch.

q: The most important biochemical investigation in thyroid enlargement is....
exp: TSH is the key biochemical test used to investigate thyroid enlargement/function.

q: The following demands re-intubation after thyroid surgery
opts: Nerve injuries to the vocal cords
exp: Airway-threatening swelling/oedema after thyroid surgery may require emergency re-intubation.

q: The following are some of the drugs used in MI EXCEPT
exp: Antibiotics are not a standard MI drug, unlike beta blockers, statins, and calcium channel blockers.

q: The critical care unit can be X shaped, U shaped or semicircle so as to have a:
exp: X/U/semicircle unit shapes allow staff good direct observation of all patients.

q: The Nurses station which is the nerve centre of the unit should be .............. to the patient.
exp: A centrally located nurses' station allows equal, easy observation of all patients.

q: This protective layer of white cheese-like substance on a baby skin after birth is....
opts: Left on the skin except the scalp
exp: Vernix caseosa is typically left on the skin (except sometimes cleaned from the scalp) as it has protective benefits.

q: In inspiration, ........................... intra pleural negative pressure must be maintained
exp: Negative intrapleural pressure is what allows the lungs to expand during inspiration.

q: Which of the following is referred to as air in the pleural space
exp: Air in the pleural space is pneumothorax.

q: Which of the following refers to blood in the pleural space?
exp: Blood in the pleural space is haemothorax.

q: The following best describes how to care for underwater-seal drainage
opts: The system is always kept below the patient's chest level
The level of water in the bottle is always below the tube from the chest
It is functional when the column of fluid is not oscillating
None of the above statements is correct.
exp: The water level in the drainage bottle must stay below the level of the chest tube to maintain the seal.

q: Which of the following statements regarding Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) are true?
opts: A GCS of 10 means the patient is in coma
Eye opening to command/speech is scored 2
exp: The maximum GCS score is 15.

q: When does primary brain injury occur?
opts: In the first hour after injury
In the first 4 hours after injury
Only if the patient is in coma
exp: Primary brain injury occurs at the moment of impact itself.

q: The following statement regarding extradural haematoma are true EXCEPT
opts: It is often caused by a skull fracture with laceration of the middle meningeal artery
It commonly occurs at the pterion
There is often a lucid interval
exp: Extradural haematoma, if treated promptly during the lucid interval, generally has a good (not poor) prognosis — making that statement false.

q: Which of the following statements regarding blunt injury are false?
opts: The mechanisms are direct or indirect.
In indirect injury, associated injuries may be present and should be sought.
Overt injury should lead the clinician to look for a covert injury as well.
In chest injuries, damage to abdominal organs is rare.
exp: Damage to abdominal organs is NOT rare in blunt chest injury — they often occur together, making that statement false.

q: Which of the following is not immediate life-threatening injuries
exp: Liver injury is a potentially (not immediately) life-threatening injury, unlike airway obstruction or pneumothorax which are immediate threats.

q: Which of the following is not potentially life-threatening injuries
exp: Aortic injury is actually classified as an immediately (not just potentially) life-threatening injury given its rapid lethality.

q: Which of the following is not a sign of acute appendicitis
exp: Murphy's sign relates to gallbladder disease, not appendicitis.

q: Your patient has developed a low-grade fever and states that she has felt very tired lately. You interpret these findings as indicating which stage of infection?
exp: Low-grade fever and fatigue before specific symptoms appear describe the prodromal stage of infection.

q: While the nurse is providing a patient personal hygiene, she observes that his skin is excessively dry. During the procedure, he tells her that he is very thirsty. An appropriate nursing diagnosis would be:
opts: Potential for impaired skin integrity related to altered gland function
Potential for impaired skin integrity related to dehydration
Impaired skin integrity related to dehydration
Impaired skin integrity related to altered circulation
exp: Dry skin and thirst point to a fluid volume deficit (dehydration) as the underlying cause.

q: All are indications for Adrenaline use EXCEPT
exp: Adrenaline is not a standard heart failure treatment, unlike its use in resuscitation, anaphylaxis, and bradycardia.

q: This is known as the fright and flight hormone
exp: Epinephrine (adrenaline) is known as the fight-or-flight hormone.

q: Use of activated charcoal is a measure taken to \...\...\...\....
opts: Limit Absorption of poisons and drugs
exp: Activated charcoal binds toxins in the gut, limiting their absorption.

q: Inj. Digoxin ampoules available contains 500 micrograms in 2ml. What volume is needed when 350 microgram is prescribed?
exp: 500mcg/2ml = 250mcg/ml, so 350mcg needs 1.4ml — wait recalculated: 350/250=1.4ml. Correction: 1.4ml is correct, matching option C actually — the closest valid answer is 1.4ml.

q: One milligram (1mg) is prescribed, tablets are 500 micrograms: how many tablets will you give?
exp: 1mg = 1000mcg; 1000mcg ÷ 500mcg per tablet = 2 tablets.

q: Poor pulmonary compliance is............\...\...
opts: A good case for underwater-seal drainage
exp: Poor lung compliance signals worsening respiratory mechanics, often prompting intubation.

q: Other indications of Endotracheal intubation may include all of the following EXCEPT:
exp: Oxygen can be given by simpler means (mask/cannula); the ETT's specific added roles are ventilation, suctioning, and specimen collection.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is defined as an absence of ventilatory support 48 hours following an extubation.
exp: Weaning success is defined as sustained breathing without support 48 hours after extubation.

q: The anaesthetic management of a patient may be described in the following phases EXCEPT
exp: "Follow up" is not one of the core anaesthetic phases, unlike preparation, induction, and maintenance.

q: Normal PaO2
exp: Normal PaO2 is approximately 75-100mmHg.

q: Which of the following is considered when choosing Analgesics for pain Management
opts: Multiple drug therapy is the corner stone.
Combine drugs with different mechanisms of action.
Consider cost, availability, safety and ease of administration.
exp: Cost, availability, safety, and ease of administration are key practical factors in choosing analgesics.

q: Which of these is Not Opioid Analgesic Drug
exp: Ketorolac is an NSAID, not an opioid, unlike morphine, pethidine, and fentanyl.

q: Which of these is not a clinical feature of mendelson's syndrome
exp: Hypotension is not a typical Mendelson's syndrome feature; it presents with respiratory symptoms like cyanosis, dyspnoea, and hypoxia.

q: The main purpose of corrugation on anaesthetic circuit is to
opts: To detect foreign body in the tube
To prevent kinking of the tube
exp: Corrugations in the anaesthetic circuit tubing prevent it from kinking and obstructing gas flow.

q: Endotracheal anaesthesia is indicated in the following except
opts: A day case where bier's block is done
A surgery where patient need awkward position
exp: A Bier's block (regional anaesthesia) day case would not require endotracheal (general) anaesthesia.

q: Causes of Brain death are EXCEPT
opts: Extracranial brain injury, as seen in patients following cardiopulmonary arrest
exp: Good brain oxygenation is the opposite of what causes brain death, unlike catastrophic injury or loss of perfusion.

q: In patients with normal cardiopulmonary status, increasing the \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ is the treatment of choice in improving the patient's oxygenation.
exp: Increasing FiO2 is the first-line way to improve oxygenation in a patient with otherwise normal cardiopulmonary status.

q: One of the following is a muscle relaxant
exp: Midazolam (via its muscle-relaxant sedative properties) is grouped here as the muscle relaxant, unlike the opioids pentazocine, fortwin, and pethidine.

q: Which of these drugs is used as an antidote for narcotics
exp: Naloxone is the antidote for narcotic (opioid) overdose.

q: which of this is a narcotic analgesics
exp: Morphine is a classic narcotic (opioid) analgesic.

q: Which of these drugs is used as an antidote for paralytic agents
exp: Neostigmine reverses non-depolarising paralytic agents by increasing acetylcholine availability.

q: \_\_\_\_\_\_ describes the present state of affairs as it exists without having any control over variables.
exp: Descriptive research describes existing conditions without manipulating variables.

q: A \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ refers to some difficulty that a researcher experiences in either a theoretical or practical situation
exp: A research problem is the difficulty/gap a researcher investigates.

q: The population to be sampled is divided into units which are known as:
exp: The units into which a population is divided for sampling are called sampling units.

q: Which of the following are steps in sampling process:
opts: Selecting and identifying the sampling method
exp: Defining the population, choosing the sampling frame, and selecting a method are all steps in the sampling process.

q: As the sample size increase, the sampling error................
exp: Larger sample sizes reduce sampling error.

q: A formal document that presents the research objectives, design of achieving these objectives and the expected outcomes/deliverables of the study is called\....
exp: A formal document outlining objectives, design, and expected outcomes is a research proposal.

q: The \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ scale measurement has a natural zero.
exp: The ratio scale is the only measurement scale with a true, meaningful zero point.

q: Which of the following is not a level of measurement in statistics?
exp: "Internal scale" is not a real level of measurement, unlike nominal, ordinal, and ratio.

q: Which is a usual criterion for abstract according to NMCN?
exp: NMCN typically requires an abstract not exceeding around 300 words.

q: Abstract should be in........................
exp: An abstract is conventionally written as a single paragraph.

q: Nutritional support for critically ill patient on admission should begin as soon as patient is \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- stable.
exp: Nutritional support should begin once the patient is haemodynamically stable.

q: The only absolute indication for Parenteral nutrition in critically-ill patients is \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
exp: Gastrointestinal (gut) failure is the one absolute indication for parenteral (bypassing the gut) nutrition.

q: When can early feeding be initiated in critically-ill patients?
exp: Early enteral feeding is generally initiated within about 24-48 hours of ICU admission.

q: Nutrition is the study of nutrients in food, how the body uses them, and the relationship between
opts: Health causes of disease in our diet
exp: Nutrition studies the relationship between diet, health, and disease.

q: When assessing the general appearances of a patient, all the following are unexpected findings EXCEPT
opts: bruising, thinning or loss of hair,
Spooning of nails, obese, or underweight.
Energetic; normal skin, hair and nails.
