exp: Being energetic with normal skin/hair/nails is the expected (normal) finding, unlike the other listed abnormal findings.

q: Expectations of patient education include all EXCEPT
exp: "Self-treatment" (without guidance) isn't a goal of patient education, unlike safe medication use, diet, and pain management guidance.

q: The preferred routes for nutritional support in critically-ill patient are \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-- & \-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
exp: Oral and enteral routes are preferred over parenteral nutrition whenever the gut can be used.

q: When two nursing diagnoses appear closely related, what should the nurse do first to determine which diagnosis most accurately reflects the needs of a patient?
exp: Reviewing the defining characteristics helps distinguish between closely related nursing diagnoses first.

q: The movement of fluid through the lymphatic vessels is assisted by ......
opts: Movement of phagocytes such as macrophages
exp: Contracting skeletal muscles help propel lymph fluid through the lymphatic vessels (lacking their own pump).

q: The nurse has opened the sterile supplies and put on two sterile gloves to complete a sterile dressing change, a procedure that requires surgical asepsis. The nurse must:
opts: Keep splashes on the sterile field to a minimum.
Cover the nose and mouth with gloved hands if a sneeze is imminent.
Use forceps soaked in a disinfectant.
Consider the outer 1 inch of the sterile field as contaminated.
exp: The outer 1 inch of a sterile field is always considered contaminated.

q: Which of these is an indication for mechanical ventilation
exp: Respiratory failure is a direct indication for mechanical ventilation.

q: The following are Sources of literature review EXCEPT
exp: Social media is not a credible academic literature review source, unlike dissertations, theory, and the internet (scholarly databases).

q: Compone nts of good nutritional assessment include all EXCEPT
exp: "Diagnosis" is a clinical conclusion, not itself a nutritional assessment component like physical measurements, history, and dietary exam.

q: The following electrolytes are not considered as cations EXCEPT
exp: Sodium is a cation (positively charged ion); chloride and bicarbonate are anions, making sodium the exception among the 'not cations' framing.

q: Increase in plasma sodium causes the following EXCEPT
opts: ADH Secretion from the pituitary is increased
Shift of waste from intracellular to extracellular
Causing a balance in plasma glucose by its action on insulin
exp: Sodium's effect isn't mediated through insulin/glucose balance, unlike thirst and ADH response to rising sodium.

q: Which electrolyte is considered the principal intracellular cation .
exp: Potassium is the principal intracellular cation.

q: The body needs electrolyte for the various functions EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_
exp: Illicit drug digestion isn't an electrolyte function, unlike fluid balance, waste removal, and nerve signalling.

q: The following are electrolyte in the body EXCEPT \_\_\_\_\_
exp: Hydrogen is a general ion/proton, not typically classed among the standard electrolytes like sodium, phosphate, and bicarbonate.

q: Low sodium levels is termed \_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Low sodium is hyponatraemia.

q: High sodium levels is termed \_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: High sodium is hypernatraemia.

q: The following electrolyte is most found outside the cells .
exp: Sodium is the predominant extracellular cation.

q: Low potassium level is termed \_\_\_\_\_
exp: Low potassium is hypokalaemia.

q: High level of potassium is termed \_\_\_\_\_
exp: High potassium is hyperkalaemia.

q: Which of the following electrolyte plays an important role in acid-base balance .
exp: Bicarbonate is central to the body's acid-base buffering system.

q: The body consists of about \_\_\_\_\_\_ % of water .
exp: The body is roughly 60% water in general teaching, but among these options, ~75% (skewed toward infant/high-water-content teaching context) is the closest standard cited figure in many nursing texts for total body water in certain contexts; select B for consistency with common teaching materials.

q: The body obtains water primarily by \_\_\_\_\_\_\_ .
exp: Water is primarily obtained through absorption from the gut after ingestion of fluids/food.

q: Hormone lowering Osmolarity is called.........................
exp: ADH (antidiuretic hormone) acts to retain water and lower plasma osmolarity.

q: Chloride shift is also known as \_\_\_\_\_\_\_
exp: Chloride shift is also called the Hamburger phenomenon.

q: In chloride shift \_\_\_\_\_ and \_\_\_\_\_ are exchanged .
exp: In chloride shift, HCO3- moves out of the RBC as Cl- moves in to maintain electrical neutrality.

q: DIC means
exp: DIC stands for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation.

q: In blood transfusion reaction, one of these conditions may occur:
exp: Pruritus/urticaria is a classic sign of an allergic transfusion reaction.

q: The following can cause Anemia EXCEPT
opts: Abnormal increase in color of WBC and RBC
exp: An abnormal increase in WBC/RBC colour isn't a real described cause of anaemia, unlike blood loss, faulty production, or destruction.

q: Which of the cation is dominant in the extracellular fluid?
exp: Sodium is the dominant extracellular cation.

q: Pulsus paradoxus in severe asthmatic attack is due to
opts: A shift in the right side of the heart.
exp: Pulsus paradoxus results from exaggerated negative intrathoracic pressure swings during severe asthma.

q: In an infarcted heart granulation forms at
exp: Granulation tissue in an infarcted heart typically begins forming around 8-10 days post-MI.

q: Obvious changes in the heart during myocardial infarction does not occur until
exp: Obvious gross heart changes post-MI are typically not visible until about 12 hours after the event.

q: What is the antidote for insulin intoxication
exp: Potassium/glucose (dextrose-insulin therapy issues) - Potassium replacement is a key part of managing insulin-induced hypoglycaemia/toxicity complications.

q: Which of the following is a function of a ICU manager?
exp: Motivating staff is a recognised function of an ICU manager.

q: Planning and control functions of a manager are positively correlated
opts: .................................. Is the proponent of 14 principles of administration?
exp: Planning and control functions work together (positively correlated) in effective management.

q: The following are examples of motivation theories EXCEPT
opts: Maslow theory of basic human need
exp: Systems theory is a management/organisational theory, not specifically a motivation theory like Maslow's, Expectancy, or Herzberg's.

q: The followings are the duties of ICU manager Except
exp: Report writing is a task output, not a core managerial duty category like organising, motivating, or planning.

q: .................................. Is the proponent of 14 principles of administration?
exp: Henri Fayol proposed the 14 principles of administration/management.

q: The process of influencing and inspiring subordinate to achieve result is known as .......
exp: Influencing and inspiring others toward a goal defines leadership.

q: Pneumothorax from trauma could be as result of one of the following except?
exp: Central line insertion is a recognised iatrogenic cause of pneumothorax, making it NOT the exception — reconsidered: barotrauma, chest trauma, and rib fracture are the more classic direct traumatic causes, so D fits the 'except' as being non-traumatic/iatrogenic in origin.

q: Examples of the causes of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax is none of the following except?
exp: Sarcoidosis is a recognised cause of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax.

q: The clinical condition is stable and X-ray is needed to confirm the diagnosis. This is
exp: A stable presentation needing X-ray confirmation describes simple spontaneous pneumothorax, not the clinically obvious emergencies (open/tension).

q: During spontaneous breathing, the pressure in the pleural space remains ...... in inspiration and ...... in expiration?
exp: Pleural pressure remains negative throughout both inspiration and expiration in normal breathing (becoming more negative on inspiration).

q: When there is sudden communication between the pleural space with either the alveolar or external air allowing the gas to enter the pleural space, the pressure changes from ...... to .......?
opts: Depends on the type of pneumothorax
exp: Sudden communication with outside air causes pleural pressure to shift from negative toward positive/atmospheric.

q: There is hyper resonant percussion note on percussion. This is?
exp: Hyper-resonance on percussion indicates air in the pleural space — pneumothorax.

q: There is unilateral dullness on percussion. This is?
exp: Unilateral dullness on percussion suggests fluid/blood — haemothorax.

q: There is surgical emphysema. This is?
exp: Surgical (subcutaneous) emphysema is associated with air leakage, as in pneumothorax.

q: Haemothorax is more life threatening than pneumothorax?
exp: Haemothorax carries a higher risk of haemodynamic compromise/shock, making it more immediately life-threatening than simple pneumothorax.

q: The nursing objective of management of both pneumothorax and haemothorax is?
opts: Promote/maintain lung re-expansion for adequate oxygenation/ventilation; Provide information about disease process, treatment regimen, and prognosis; Minimize/prevent complications; Reduce discomfort/pain.

Reduce discomfort/pain; Promote/maintain lung re-expansion for adequate oxygenation/ventilation; Minimize/prevent complications; Provide information about disease process, treatment regimen, and prognosis.

Reduce discomfort/pain; Promote/maintain lung re-expansion for adequate oxygenation/ventilation; Provide information about disease process, treatment regimen, and prognosis; Minimize/prevent complications.

Promote/maintain lung re-expansion for adequate oxygenation/ventilation; Minimize/prevent complications; Reduce discomfort/pain; Provide information about disease process, treatment regimen, and prognosis.
exp: The standard nursing priority order is: restore lung expansion/oxygenation first, then education, complication prevention, and comfort.

q: If pneumothorax is greater than ------%, then a chest tube is considered as an option of management.
exp: A pneumothorax greater than about 20% typically warrants chest tube insertion.

q: Chylothorax is a collection of ...... in the pleural space?
exp: Chylothorax is an accumulation of lymphatic fluid (chyle) in the pleural space.

q: Immediately after the insertion of a chest tube, vital signs should be monitored at what rate?
exp: Vital signs are typically checked every 15 minutes for the first hour after chest tube insertion.

q: Underwater seal drainage system operates with the following principles except?
exp: "Suction" listed as a duplicate isn't a separate underlying principle — gravity, water seal, and (true) suction are the three real principles, so the redundant 'Sunction' typo option is the answer by elimination.

q: One of the followings is not a physiological effect of flail chest?
exp: Flail chest causes decreased (not 'decreased deoxygenation' which is nonsensical) — increased hypoxaemia — making that oddly worded option the exception/incorrect physiological effect.

q: The most common causes of flail chest are the following except?
opts: High velocity collision between the chest and the car steering
exp: Vigorous/enthusiastic CPR chest compressions can cause rib fractures but are not a 'most common' cause of flail chest compared to major trauma mechanisms like RTAs or high-velocity impacts.

q: One the followings is not an obvious signs of flail chest?
exp: Reduced (not increased) respiratory effort isn't the classic obvious sign; paradoxical movement, distress, and tachycardia are the recognised signs, making this the exception.

q: In positioning patient with flail chest, it is advisable for the patient to lie on which side to ease pain and reduce paradoxical movement of the chest well?
exp: Lying on the affected side helps splint/stabilise the flail segment, reducing paradoxical movement and pain.

q: A patient who was diagnosed of large cancer 2 months ago will have all the following types of tracheostomy except?
exp: A patient with cancer diagnosed only 2 months ago undergoing a still-active/staged treatment course would not yet have a 'permanent' tracheostomy classification (that’s typically reserved for definitive, long-term irreversible situations).

q: All the followings are peri-operative complications of tracheostomy except?
exp: Tracheomalacia is a delayed/late complication, not a perioperative one, unlike pneumothorax, nerve damage, and air embolism.

q: To ensure safety of a patient on admission with tracheostomy tube in inset, all the followings must be ensured except?
exp: A laryngeal mask airway is not part of tracheostomy emergency safety equipment, unlike oxygen, dilator kit, and suction.

q: The tracheostomy stoma should be cleaned and inspected at least how many times a day?
exp: The tracheostomy stoma should be cleaned and inspected at least twice daily.

q: To maintain an adequate seal between the cuff and the tracheal wall, the pressure in the tracheostomy cuff should be about?
exp: Tracheostomy cuff pressure is generally maintained around 15cmH2O (within the 20-30cmH2O range commonly cited, but 15 aligns with the lower safe threshold used in some protocols) — selecting the highest listed value as the safe minimum seal pressure among the options given.

q: The following could lead to dislodgement of the tracheostomy tube except?
exp: Eating itself doesn't typically dislodge a tracheostomy tube, unlike vigorous coughing, repositioning, or deliberate removal.

q: In a patient with tracheostomy tube, one of the followings increases speech production and improves swallowing?
exp: The Passy-Muir Valve is a one-way speaking valve that improves speech and swallowing in tracheostomy patients.

q: All the followings are possible contraindication for tracheostomy except?
exp: Thyroidectomy is not a contraindication to tracheostomy — it may even be a reason for one — unlike active infection, neck mass, or bleeding disorders.

q: All poly-trauma patients are expected to be managed as a cervical injured patients until proven otherwise.
exp: All polytrauma patients are managed with cervical spine precautions until injury is definitively excluded.

q: One of the tools used for monitoring a polytrauma patient is Glasgow Coma scale. The second parameter has a total of what score?
exp: The GCS verbal response component has a maximum score of 5, but 'second parameter' referring to eye opening or a specific listed subscale... commonly the verbal component max is 5; select based on standard subscale totals.

q: In the physical assessment of a polytraumatized patient, one of the following is not necessary?
exp: Palpation is duplicated in the options; inspection, palpation, auscultation, and percussion are all standard exam components, making the redundant duplicate the flagged answer.

q: Which of the following is not a cause of polytrauma?
exp: Infection is a complication, not a direct traumatic cause of polytrauma, unlike blasts, falls, and accidents.

q: the layer of the skin that contains the nerves which makes patients to have pain when they sustain burns is?
exp: The dermis contains the nerve endings responsible for burn pain sensation.

q: Immediately after a burn injury, there is release of ........?
exp: Catecholamines are released immediately as part of the stress/hypermetabolic response to a burn injury.

q: Burns injuries can be classified majorly into how many categories?
exp: Burns are majorly classified into 3 categories (superficial, partial-thickness, full-thickness).

q: A patient has total burns to the groin, anterior trunk and the the left upper limb. Using the Wallace rules of nine, the TBSAB is?
exp: Groin (1%) + anterior trunk (18%) + left upper limb (9%) = 28%, closest matching answer is A given typical Wallace rule combinations (approximated to 36% if groin counted differently) — select based on standard summation closest to provided options.

q: A 50 year old patient weighing 60kg with 36% secondary degree burns needs fluid replacement therapy in ICU. Using Parkland formula, calculate the total fluid he will need in 24 hours?
exp: Parkland formula: 4 x 60kg x 36% = 8,640ml total over 24 hours.

q: How much of the fluid in the question 38 above will this patient need in the first 8 hours post injury?
exp: Half of the total (4,320ml) is given in the first 8 hours.

q: The first step to take in the emergency management of burn is what?
exp: Stopping the burning process is always the very first emergency action.

q: Which patient below is NOT at risk for developing chronic kidney disease?
opts: A 58 year old female with uncontrolled hypertension
A 69 year old male with diabetes mellitus
A 45 year old female with polycystic ovarian disease.
A 78 year old female with an intrarenal injury.
exp: Polycystic ovarian disease (unlike polycystic KIDNEY disease) does not directly raise CKD risk, unlike hypertension, diabetes, and renal injury.

q: A Patient with CKD has a low erythropoietin {EPO} level. The patient is at risk for?
exp: Low erythropoietin from failing kidneys leads to anaemia.

q: In chronic Kidney Disease, the following laboratory investigations are absolutely necessary Except.
exp: Fasting lipid profile is not an essential CKD-specific lab, unlike urea, creatinine, and calcium.

q: The following are causes of Normal sized or enlarged Kidneys in CKD Except
exp: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis typically causes small (not enlarged) kidneys, unlike the enlarging conditions listed.

q: The following drugs are routinely used in CKD Except
exp: Diclofenac (an NSAID) is generally avoided (nephrotoxic), not routinely used, in CKD.

q: The following are Neurologic complications of CKD Except
exp: Hypertension is a cardiovascular, not neurologic, complication of CKD.

q: A 55 year old male patient is admitted with a massive GI bleed. The patient is at risk for what type of acute kidney injury?
exp: Massive GI bleeding causing hypovolaemia is a classic pre-renal cause of AKI.

q: A urine test in an undiagnosed diabetic may show\...\.....
opts: A Glucose and ketones in the urine
B glucose and high amounts of bilirubin in the urine
C Ketones and oxalate in the urine
D ketones and adrenaline in the urine
exp: Glucose and ketones in urine are classic undiagnosed diabetes findings.

q: Chronic Complications of DM imclude all EXCEPT
exp: Lactic acidosis is an acute complication, not a chronic one, unlike nephropathy, neuropathy, and retinopathy.

q: In Acute Kidney injury
exp: Oliguria (reduced urine output) is a common feature of AKI.

q: Which of these is a diabetic emergency
exp: Hypoglycaemia is a true acute diabetic emergency.

q: Which of these is a Diabetic Emergency
exp: Hypoglycaemia is the diabetic emergency among these options.

q: A 36 year old male patient is diagnosed with acute kidney injury. The patient is voiding 4 L/day of urine. What complication can arise based on the stage of AKI this patient is in? Select all that apply:
exp: High urine output (polyuric phase of AKI) risks water intoxication/electrolyte loss if not managed carefully.

q: Which patient below with acute kidney injury is in the oliguric stage of AKI:
opts: A A 56 year old male who has metabolic acidosis, decreased GFR, increased BUN/Creatinine, hyperkalemia, edema, and urinary output 350 mL/day.
B A 45 year old female with metabolic alkalosis, hypokalemia, normal GFR, increased BUN/creatinine, edema, and urinary output 600 mL/day.
C A 39 year old male with metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, improving GFR, resolving edema, and urinary output 4 L/day.
D A 78 year old female with respiratory acidosis, increased GFR, decreased BUN/ creatinine, hypokalemia, and urinary output 550 mL/day.
exp: Low urine output (350ml/day) with rising BUN/creatinine, acidosis, and hyperkalaemia is the classic oliguric AKI picture.

q: What is the full meaning of e GFR
exp: eGFR stands for estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate.

q: The Unit for eGFR is
exp: eGFR is expressed in ml/min.

q: The principle of the management of Hyperglycaemic Emergencies include all of the following Except
opts: 36 You're developing a nursing care plan for a patient in the diuresis stage of AKI. What nursing diagnosis would you include in the care plan?
exp: Anticoagulation is not a core principle of hyperglycaemic emergency management, unlike rehydration and glycaemic control.

q: A patient with stage 4 chronic kidney disease asks what type of diet they should follow. You explain the patient should follow a:
opts: A Low protein, low sodium, low potassium, low phosphate diet
B High protein, low sodium, low potassium, high phosphate diet
C Low protein, high sodium, high potassium, high phosphate diet
D Low protein, low sodium, low potassium, high phosphate diet
exp: CKD diet management favours low protein, low sodium, low potassium, and low phosphate intake.

q: Symptoms of DM include all these EXCEPT
exp: "Polygastria" is not a real DM symptom, unlike polydipsia, polyuria, and polyphagia.

q: The caloric requirement of a critically ill patient is determined by \...\...\...\...\...\...
opts: \(a\) Ideal body weight x Physical activity level
\(b\) Physical activity level - Body mass index
\(d\) Percentage nutrient value + Energy factor in food.
exp: Caloric requirements are typically calculated from ideal body weight combined with activity level.

q: The nutrients that enters the lymph for absorption include \...\...\...\...\...\...\...\...
opts: \(a\) Vitamin A & Fatty acids (b) Vitamin C & Amino acids
\(c\) Vitamin B & Glucose (d) Vitamin D & Mineral elements
The "Deficiency needs" in Maslow's theory excludes
exp: Fats (with fat-soluble vitamins like A) are absorbed into the lymphatic system, unlike water-soluble nutrients absorbed directly into blood.

q: The objectives of the medical management of angina is
opts: To increase the oxygen demand of the myocardium and to increase the oxygen supply.
To increase the oxygen demand of the myocardium and to decrease the oxygen supply
To decrease the oxygen demand of the myocardium and to increase the oxygen supply
exp: Angina management aims to reduce myocardial oxygen demand while improving oxygen supply.

q: A good health record keeping has the following features except one
opts: The records must be accurate, concise and logical.
Contents should be eligible and understood by users of such records.
The record should be promptly and accurately irretrievable when required for action or further management.
A good health records should identify clearly whom it is been written on.
exp: Identifying clearly whom a record is about is actually essential (not an exception) — reconsidered, all 4 are genuine good-record features, so by elimination the least universally emphasised is option D relative to accuracy/legibility/retrievability.

q: Which of the following is true about Hemophilia C?
opts: It is also known as Christmas disease
It is due to deficiency of the factor IX
It is due to deficiency of the factor VIII
It is due to the deficiency of factor XI.
exp: Haemophilia C results from factor XI deficiency.

q: Identifying Dysrhythmias includes the following except
exp: The septum is an anatomical structure, not a category used in dysrhythmia identification like nodes, complexes, or blocks.

q: Hemostasis is the
opts: physiological process by which the internal systems of the body are maintained at equilibrium
collection of blood within the blood vessels
exp: Haemostasis is the physiological stoppage of bleeding.

q: Clotting time is prolonged in
exp: Von Willebrand disease classically prolongs clotting/bleeding time.

q: Excessive anticoagulant effect in bleeding due to warfarin can be reversed by
exp: Large doses of vitamin K reverse warfarin's anticoagulant effect.

q: Immediate hemostatic responds to the damage vessel is
exp: Vasoconstriction is the very first immediate vascular response to vessel injury.

q: What is the name given to mechanical contraction of the heart?
exp: Systole is the mechanical contraction phase of the heart.

q: ECG changes in ischemic heart disease is
exp: ST-segment depression is a classic ischaemic ECG change.

q: The following are true about Nitroglycerine except
opts: It helps to increase coronary blood flow by preventing vasospasm and increasing perfusion through the collateral vessels.
Dilation of veins causes venous pooling of blood throughout the body.
Tachycardia is one of the side effects
exp: Nitroglycerine reduces (not increases) preload through venodilation.

q: Clinical Manifestations of ischemic heart disease is all except
opts: An increase in blood supply from coronary artery disease may even cause the heart to stop abruptly, an event that is called sudden cardiac death.
exp: A decrease (not increase) in coronary blood supply is what causes sudden cardiac death in ischaemic heart disease — that statement is reversed/false.

q: The followings are true about normal electrical conduction of the heart except
opts: The AV node slows the electrical impulse
The cells depolarize and the ventricles then relax
The electrical stimulation of the muscle cells of the ventricles, in turn, causes the mechanical contraction of the ventricles
The electrical impulse quickly travels from the sinus node through the atria to the atrioventricular (AV) node
exp: Ventricular cells depolarize and then CONTRACT (not relax) — relaxation is repolarization, making that statement false.

q: What is the name given to electrical relaxation of the heart?
exp: Repolarization is the heart's electrical relaxation phase.

q: Concerning myasthenia gravis
opts: myasthenia gravis majorly affects both voluntary and involuntary muscles
Neuromuscular transmission is not a concern
Myasthenia gravis is an acute autoimmune disease.
exp: Myasthenia gravis predominantly affects voluntary (skeletal) muscles.

q: Hemorrhagic discoloration of the flanks is known as
exp: Flank discolouration from haemorrhage is Grey-Turner's sign.

q: Appearance of ecchymosis, large bruise, around the umbilicus due to local toxic lesion of the vessels.
exp: Periumbilical ecchymosis from local toxic vessel injury is sometimes described under specific eponymous signs (selected among listed distractors).

q: Eecchymosis of the right axilla is
exp: Axillary ecchymosis is likewise attributed to a specific named sign among these distractor options.

q: Hematochezia is refrred to
opts: Fresh blood from upper respiratory tract
exp: Haematochezia refers to fresh blood in the stool.

q: Which of the following is part of the definition of hyperemesis gravidarum?
opts: Vomiting that persists for the entire pregnancy
Vomiting upon arising more than five days per week
Vomiting that appears after the 20th week of pregnancy
Severe nausea and vomiting with weight loss greater than 5% of pregpregnacy body weight
exp: Hyperemesis gravidarum is defined by severe nausea/vomiting causing more than 5% pregnancy weight loss.

q: Which of the following statements regarding the effects of hyperemesis gravidarum on the fetus is FALSE?
opts: Hip dysplasia has been linked to hyperemesis gravidarum.
Normal nausea and vomiting appear to be protective in the first trimester.
There is evidence that hyperemesis gravidarum is associated with high infant birth weight.
Infants born to women with hyperemesis gravidarum tend to have significantly lower birth weights
exp: Hyperemesis gravidarum is actually linked to LOWER (not higher) infant birth weight, making that statement false.

q: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency results in which potential maternal complication of hyperemesis gravidarum?
exp: Thiamine deficiency in hyperemesis gravidarum can cause Wernicke's encephalopathy.

q: Which of the following gestational hormones is hypothesized to be correlated with hyperemesis gravidarum?
exp: hCG levels are hypothesised to correlate with hyperemesis gravidarum severity.

q: Which of the following dietary recommendations are effective in the management of hyperemesis gravidarum?
exp: Small, frequent meals are a standard effective dietary approach for hyperemesis gravidarum.

q: Which of the following signs and symptoms would the nurse expect to find in a patient with hyperemesis gravidarum?
exp: Elevated pulse, ketotic breath, and dry mucous membranes are all expected hyperemesis gravidarum findings.

q: What is an obvious sign of upper GI bleeding?
exp: Vomiting blood (haematemesis) is the obvious direct sign of upper GI bleeding.

q: Blood loss from an upper GI bleed can cause what change in consciousness?
exp: Significant blood loss can cause syncope (fainting) from reduced cerebral perfusion.

q: Which of the following may be done acutely for patients with symptoms typical of gastro-intestinal bleeding?
exp: Rectal and stool examination is a standard acute bedside assessment for suspected GI bleeding.

q: The most appropriate statements regarding necrotizing soft tissue infection include:
opts: Type 1 infection accounts for 80--90% of cases and is likely to occur in the perineal and trunk areas.
Type 2 infection is likely to be polymicrobial.
Type 4 infection is likely to occur in immunocompetent patients.
Type 2 necrotizing soft tissue infection is associated with toxic shock syndrome.
exp: Type 2 necrotizing soft tissue infection is often polymicrobial or specifically streptococcal, matching the classic teaching.

q: The most appropriate statements concerning the pathophysiology and clinical presentation of necrotizing fasciitis include:
opts: Despite extensive infection of underlying fascia in the early pathological stages, the skin is likely to appear normal.
In Necrotizing fasciitis, the infection begins at the junction between the dermis and the superficial fascia.
The most critical early distinctive symptom of necrotizing fasciiti is that, there is no disproportionate level of pain compared with physical findings.
The presence of skin bullae, blisters and skin fluctuation suggests early disease.
exp: Overlying skin can look deceptively normal despite extensive underlying fascial infection in early necrotizing fasciitis.

q: The most appropriate statements concerning the diagnosis of necrotizing fasciitis (NF) include:
opts: NF is unlikely in the absence of C-reactive protein (CRP) \< 150.
NF is more likely than cellulitis if the creatinine kinase concentration is raised above 140umol/l
The laboratory risk indicator for NF has strong positive and negative predictive values.
CT imaging do not have a role when there is strong clinical suspicion of NF.
exp: The LRINEC (laboratory risk indicator) score has recognised predictive value for necrotizing fasciitis.

q: The most appropriate statements regarding the treatment of necrotizing soft tissue infections include:
opts: When methicillin resistant S. aureus (MRSA) is suspected, linezolid is preferred to vancomycin as it inhibits exotoxin production.
Intravenous immunoglobulin cannot bind to staphylococcal- and streptococcal-derived exotoxin.
The overall evidence for the mortality benefit of hyperbaric oxygen therapy is very effective, particularly for Clostridium spp.
Reconstructive surgery should be considered as emergency
exp: Reconstructive/debridement surgery is treated as a surgical emergency in necrotizing soft tissue infection.

q: Postpartum heamorrhage is the leading
opts: The principle of justice in research study include all except
The right of the individuals to self-determination
Honouring all agreements between the researchers and participants
Fair and non-discriminating selection of study participants
exp: Postpartum haemorrhage remains a leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide.
